concepts.title: Vertex operators and locality
concepts.blurb: A vertex operator $Y(a, z) = \sum_{n} a_n z^{-n-1}$ is a formal Laurent series of operators on a state space $V$ assigned to each $a \in V$; the axiom of locality $(z-w)^N [Y(a,z), Y(b,w)] = 0$ for $N \gg 0$ replaces commutativity with mutual asymptotic vanishing.

concepts.title: VOA axioms — vacuum, translation, locality, Jacobi
concepts.blurb: A vertex operator algebra is $(V, Y, \mathbf{1}, \omega)$ satisfying vacuum ($Y(\mathbf{1}, z) = \mathrm{id}$), translation ($[T, Y(a,z)] = \partial_z Y(a,z)$), locality, and the Borcherds–Jacobi identity that ties operator-product expansion to a single equation; all four are equivalent characterisations under mild hypotheses.

concepts.title: Virasoro algebra and central charge
concepts.blurb: The conformal vector $\omega \in V$ produces operators $L_n$ with $[L_m, L_n] = (m-n) L_{m+n} + \tfrac{c}{12}(m^3 - m)\,\delta_{m+n,0}$; the central charge $c$ is a numerical invariant of the VOA and $L_0$ supplies the integer grading that turns $V$ into a graded module.

concepts.title: Modular invariance — Zhu's theorem
concepts.blurb: For a $C_2$-cofinite rational VOA, the graded character $\mathrm{ch}_M(\tau) = \tr_M q^{L_0 - c/24}$ of an irreducible module is a component of a vector-valued modular form for $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$; Zhu's theorem (1996) is the bridge between two-dimensional CFT and modular forms.

concepts.title: The moonshine module $V^\natural$
concepts.blurb: Frenkel–Lepowsky–Meurman constructed $V^\natural$ — a $\mathbb{Z}$-graded VOA of central charge $24$ whose graded dimension is $J(\tau) = j(\tau) - 744$ and whose automorphism group is the Monster; Borcherds' proof of Conway–Norton uses the Monster Lie algebra built from $V^\natural$ via the Goddard–Thorn no-ghost theorem.

concepts.title: Applications — chiral CFT and chiral algebras
concepts.blurb: VOAs axiomatise the chiral half of two-dimensional conformal field theory; Beilinson–Drinfeld's chiral algebras globalise the construction over algebraic curves, replacing formal power series with $\mathcal{D}$-modules and giving the framework underlying the geometric Langlands program.
